issues.body: The header asserts this 'runs only after the batcher closes an omnibus', but nothing enforces that. The only guards are `if: startsWith(github.event.pull_request.head.ref, 'sparq-omnibus/')` and a marker-comment check. For a fork PR, `head.ref` is the fork's branch name and is fully attacker-controlled, so naming a branch `sparq-omnibus/x` passes the gate. The marker `sparq-omnibus-failure:v1` is checked in issue comments (`comments.some(...)`), and any GitHub user can post that comment on their own PR, so it is also forgeable. `pull_request_target: [closed]` fires for fork PRs (merged or not) with the base-repo token and the declared `issues: write` + `actions: write`. Result: an external contributor can trigger repo issue creation (spam) via `issues.create`, and rerun base-repo Actions jobs via POST .../jobs/{job_id}/rerun. Unlike the repo's other pull_request_target workflow (bead-autoclose.yml), this one has no same-repo / trusted-author / merged guard. No secret exfil or code-exec (no PR checkout), but the write-scoped token is reachable by untrusted actors.
issues.fix_hint: Add a trusted-provenance guard to the job `if`: require the PR to be same-repo, e.g. `github.event.pull_request.head.repo.full_name == github.repository` (fork branches cannot forge this), and consider gating on `github.event.pull_request.merged == true` since only merged omnibuses have a real merge-group head. Optionally verify the marker comment was authored by the batcher account rather than trusting any comment body.
issues.title: pull_request_target gate is forgeable by fork PRs -> unauthorized issue creation and job reruns

issues.body: Each failed leg is processed sequentially, and for each the code polls up to 30 times with a 15s sleep (`for (let attempt = 0; attempt < 30; attempt++) { setTimeout(..., 15000) }`) = up to 7.5 minutes of waiting per leg before giving up. `timeout-minutes: 20` means 3+ slow legs (or 2 legs whose reruns are slow) can blow the timeout. Because the check-run rename (`PATCH .../check-runs`) happens inside the per-leg loop but the tracking-issue creation and the PR comment happen only AFTER the loop, a mid-loop timeout can rename some checks to `advisory` while filing no issue and posting no comment — a silently degraded outcome where quarantined legs have no audit trail.
issues.fix_hint: Probe legs concurrently (Promise.all over the failed legs) rather than serially, and/or bound total wait against the job timeout. Persist/append the tracking issue incrementally (or create it before the rename loop) so a timeout still leaves a traceable record of what was quarantined.
issues.title: Sequential per-leg polling can exceed the 20-minute job timeout, leaving partial quarantine with no tracking issue

issues.body: Replacement detection is `checks.filter(c => c.name === check.name && !initialIds.has(c.id))`. This only works if re-running a single job via POST /actions/jobs/{job_id}/rerun produces a check-run with a NEW id at the same head SHA. If GitHub updates the existing check-run in place (same id, new attempt), the `!initialIds.has(c.id)` predicate is never satisfied, the loop always times out with 'did not conclude', and no leg is ever quarantined — the workflow silently no-ops. This is the load-bearing assumption of the whole mechanism and is not defended by a test.
issues.fix_hint: Confirm empirically that a single-job rerun yields a fresh check-run id at the same SHA. If it can update in place, detect the flip by comparing the check-run's updated_at / started_at against the initial run, or by the run attempt number, instead of relying on id novelty.
issues.title: Rerun-replacement detection depends on the reran job producing a NEW check-run id

issues.body: The quarantine step does `PATCH /repos/{owner}/{repo}/check-runs/{check_run_id}` with the workflow GITHUB_TOKEN. The Checks API only permits a check-run to be updated by the GitHub App that created it. The targeted legs are native Actions jobs (guaranteed by the details_url regex), which are created by the GitHub Actions app; the workflow token is the same app installation, so this likely works — but this is not obviously guaranteed across all leg producers and would 403 (throwing the step) if any targeted check-run was created by a different integration. Worth verifying, since a 403 aborts the run before the issue/comment is filed.
issues.fix_hint: Wrap the PATCH in a try/catch so a single non-updatable check-run does not abort the whole quarantine pass, and confirm the token can rename Actions-created check-runs in practice.
issues.title: check-runs PATCH rename may be rejected for check-runs the token's app did not create

issues.body: The workflow declares `pull-requests: write`, but the PR interaction is a comment via `issues.createComment` (PR comments are issue comments, covered by `issues: write`). No pulls API mutation is performed. Least-privilege suggests dropping `pull-requests: write`.
issues.fix_hint: Remove `pull-requests: write` from the permissions block unless a genuine pulls-API write is added.
issues.title: Over-broad permissions (pull-requests: write appears unused)

issues.body: The merge-group run selection accepts conclusions ['failure','timed_out','cancelled'], but the per-leg `failed` filter only accepts ['failure','timed_out']. A run selected because it was 'cancelled' may therefore contribute no rerunnable legs, and cancelled legs are never probed for flakiness even though cancellation is a common flake symptom. Harmless but inconsistent.
issues.fix_hint: Align the two conclusion sets, or document why cancelled legs are intentionally excluded from re-probing.
issues.title: Inconsistent conclusion sets: 'cancelled' selects the merge-group run but is never re-probed as a leg

verdict.summary: New non-gating observer workflow that reruns failed omnibus merge-group legs once and renames green-flip check-runs with the whole-word `advisory` token so a future v2 bisection collector skips them. The `advisory` rename premise is sound (verified against .github/E2E-GATING-POLICY.md and the ci-summary aggregator), and the marker string matches scripts/batch-merge.py. Not checking out PR code is the right call for pull_request_target. However the trigger is guarded ONLY by a forgeable branch-name prefix plus a forgeable marker comment, so an external fork PR author can drive this write-scoped token (issues:write / actions:write) to spam issues and rerun base-repo CI jobs. Separately, the sequential per-leg polling (up to 7.5 min each) can exceed the 20-minute job timeout and leave a partial quarantine with no tracking issue. These are the blocking concerns; a few least-privilege / robustness nits follow. Recommend request_changes.
